questions.question: What is the formal definition of f(n) = O(g(n))?
questions.answer: There exist constants c > 0 and n0 >= 1 such that for all n >= n0, f(n) <= c*g(n). This means g(n) is an upper bound on f(n) beyond some threshold.

questions.question: What is amortised analysis and how does it differ from worst-case analysis?
questions.answer: Amortised measures the average cost per operation over a sequence of n operations -- a guarantee, not a probability. Worst-case measures a single operation worst possible cost. Dynamic array append is O(n) worst-case but O(1) amortised because resizes are rare.

questions.question: Apply the Master Theorem to T(n) = 2T(n/2) + O(n). Show which case applies and state the result.
questions.answer: a=2, b=2, f(n)=n. n^log_b(a) = n^1 = n. Since f(n) = Theta(n^log_b(a)), Case 2 applies, giving T(n) = Theta(n log n).

questions.question: If n <= 10^5, which is the slowest algorithm complexity that still passes within 1 second?
questions.explanation: O(n log n) produces ~1.7x10^6 operations for n=10^5, well within the ~10^8 budget. O(n^2) gives 10^10 -- TLE.

questions.question: Which statement about Theta notation is correct?

options.A: f(n)=Theta(g(n)) means f is only an upper bound of g
options.B: f(n)=Theta(g(n)) means f grows both no faster and no slower than g
options.D: Theta notation is equivalent to O notation

questions.explanation: Theta is a tight bound: f=Theta(g) means f=O(g) AND f=Omega(g). It characterises the exact growth rate.
